Raffles Girls’ Primary School 2021 P6 Science Preliminary Examination

Date: 20 August 2021  |  Duration: 1 hour 45 minutes  |  Total: 100 marks

Booklet A — Multiple Choice Questions (56 marks)

For each question from 1 to 28, four options are given. One of them is the correct answer. Each question carries 2 marks.

Question 1

2 marks

Sam spotted a staghorn fern and mushrooms, as shown in the diagram below.

Staghorn fern

Mushrooms

Which of the following is correct about both the staghorn fern and the mushrooms?

  • A  Both reproduce from spores.
  • B  Both are non-flowering plants.
  • C  Both can make their own food.
  1. A only
  2. C only
  3. A and B only
  4. B and C only

Answer

Option 1. Both the staghorn fern and mushrooms reproduce from spores (A is true). Mushrooms are fungi, not plants, and cannot make their own food (C is false). Mushrooms are also not non-flowering plants in the plant kingdom sense, so B is false. Only statement A is correct for both organisms.

Question 2

2 marks

Sarah bought a toy doll that came with a feeding bottle as shown below.

She fed the toy doll daily and recorded the height of the doll weekly. Which of the following graphs correctly shows the results over four weeks?

(Graph options shown in the original paper — the toy doll is not a living thing and does not grow, so its height remains constant over time.)

  1. Graph showing height increasing over weeks
  2. Graph showing height decreasing over weeks
  3. Graph showing height fluctuating over weeks
  4. Graph showing height remaining constant (flat line) over weeks

Answer

Option 4. A toy doll is not a living thing and does not possess the life process of growth. No matter how much it is "fed", its height will remain the same over the four weeks. The correct graph shows a flat (constant) horizontal line.

Question 3

2 marks

The diagram below shows the development of a plant at different stages namely A, B and C.

At which stage(s) do/does the seedling only depend on its seed leaves for food?

  1. A only
  2. A and B only
  3. B and C only
  4. A, B and C

Answer

Option 1. The seedling depends solely on the food stored in its seed leaves (cotyledons) only at the earliest stage of germination (stage A), before it develops true leaves. Once true leaves appear, the plant can photosynthesise to make its own food and is no longer solely dependent on the seed leaves.

Question 4

2 marks

The graph below shows the changes in food intake by an insect throughout its life cycle.

Based on the information above, which part of the graph represents the larval stage of the insect?

  1. BC
  2. CD
  3. DE
  4. EF

Answer

Option 1. The larval stage is characterised by very high and increasing food intake as the larva grows rapidly and stores energy for metamorphosis. Section BC shows the highest food intake, representing the larval stage. The other sections represent egg (AB), pupa (CD/DE — little or no intake), and adult (EF — lower intake).

Question 5

2 marks

Which of the following is not an inherited characteristic?

  1. fair-skin
  2. long hair
  3. sharp nose
  4. blue-coloured eyes

Answer

Option 2. Long hair is an acquired characteristic — it is determined by personal choice and grooming, not by genes inherited from parents. Fair skin (1), sharp nose (3) and blue-coloured eyes (4) are all determined by genetic inheritance from parents.

Question 6

2 marks

The diagram shows a human reproductive system.

Which of the following statement(s) is/are true when only part X was removed?

  • A  No egg cells will be produced.
  • B  A baby can still develop in her reproductive system.
  • C  Fertilisation can still occur in her reproductive system.
  1. A only
  2. C only
  3. A and B only
  4. B and C only

Answer

Option 4. Part X is one ovary. If only part X (one ovary) is removed, the other ovary remains functional and can still produce egg cells. Fertilisation can still occur in the fallopian tube (C is true). A baby can still develop in the uterus (B is true). Statement A is false because the remaining ovary still produces eggs.

Question 7

2 marks

Which of the following is correct?

(1)

(2)

(3)

(4)

Answer

Option 2. The correct diagram shows the relationship between plants and animals in terms of gas exchange: plants take in carbon dioxide and release oxygen during photosynthesis, while animals take in oxygen and release carbon dioxide during respiration. Option 2 correctly represents this interdependence.

Question 8

2 marks

The diagram (not drawn to scale) below shows two pods, X and Y, dispersing their seeds when the pods split.

X

Y

The diagrams below show the distribution of young plants observed over a period of time.

Diagram 1

Diagram 2

Key: ▲ Parent plant X   △ Young plant X   ● Parent plant Y   ○ Young plant Y   → Wind direction

Based on the information provided above, which of the following is/are definitely true?

  • A  Seeds of fruit X are lighter than seeds of fruit Y.
  • B  Seeds of fruit Y are inedible but seeds of fruit X are edible.
  • C  Seeds of fruit X can stay afloat in the air longer and be displaced further.
  • D  Seeds of fruit X are dispersed by wind only while seeds of fruit Y are dispersed by splitting action only.
  1. C only
  2. A and C only
  3. B and D only
  4. A, B, and D only

Answer

Option 1. From the diagrams, the young plants of X are spread further in the direction of the wind, showing seeds of X are carried further by wind. This means seeds of X can stay afloat longer and be displaced further (C is definitely true). A cannot be confirmed — lighter seeds would travel further but the diagrams do not directly measure mass. B and D make unwarranted assumptions not supported by the given information. Only C is definitely true.

Question 9

2 marks

The diagram shows the functions of some parts of the human digestive system.

Which of the following statements about parts A, B, C and D is correct?

PartStatement
(1)ANo digestion takes place
(2)BDigests food completely
(3)CDigests food completely and absorbs it into the bloodstream
(4)DAbsorbs digested food and water

Answer

Option 3. Part C is the small intestine. It is where digestion of food is completed (by enzymes from the pancreas and intestinal lining) and where digested nutrients are absorbed into the bloodstream through the villi. Options 1, 2 and 4 are incorrect: the mouth/stomach (A and B) do not complete digestion, and part D (large intestine) absorbs mainly water, not digested food.

Question 10

2 marks

The diagram shows two plants, P and Q. Plant P grows in the water and plant Q grows on land.

Which is/are the main function(s) of the roots of both plants P and Q?

  • A  To store food
  • B  To take in water and nutrients
  • C  To hold a plant down in the soil
  1. B only
  2. A and B only
  3. B and C only
  4. A, B, and C

Answer

Option 1. The main function of roots common to both the water plant P and land plant Q is to take in water and nutrients (B). Plant P grows in water and may not anchor in soil (C does not apply to both). Not all plants store food in their roots (A is not a universal main function). Therefore B only is correct for both plants.

Question 11

2 marks

The diagram below shows two cells, A and B, examined under the microscope.

Which of the following correctly identifies cells A and B and the function of part X?

Cell ACell BFunction of part X
(1)plantplantcontrol all activities within the cell
(2)plantanimalcontrol all activities within the cell
(3)plantplantcaptures sunlight for the plants to make food
(4)plantanimalcaptures sunlight for the plants to make food

Answer

Option 2. Cell A has a cell wall, chloroplasts and a large vacuole — it is a plant cell. Cell B has no cell wall or chloroplasts — it is an animal cell. Part X is the nucleus (visible in both), whose function is to control all activities within the cell. Option 2 correctly identifies both cell types and the function of the nucleus.

Question 12

2 marks

Clara prepared an experimental set-up by placing a plant under strong light as shown below.

After eight hours, she removed leaf X from the plant and tested for the presence of starch. She placed leaf X in a beaker of boiling water for twenty minutes and then placed the leaf on a white tile as shown below. She added a few drops of yellowish-brown iodine solution on the whole leaf.

She recorded her observations in the table below. Based on the information above, which of the following is correct?

Colour of iodine solution on part of leaf X inside the bottleColour of iodine solution on part of leaf X outside the bottle
(1)Yellowish-brownDark blue
(2)Yellowish-brownYellowish-brown
(3)Dark blueYellowish-brown
(4)Dark blueDark blue

Answer

Option 1. The bottle was sealed over part of leaf X, trapping carbon dioxide inside (from the plant's own respiration) but blocking further CO₂ from outside. The part of the leaf inside the bottle lacked access to CO₂ and could not photosynthesise, so no starch was produced — iodine remains yellowish-brown. The part outside had access to CO₂ and light, so it photosynthesised and produced starch — iodine turns dark blue.

Question 13

2 marks

Volcano scientists wear lava suits to work close to hot molten lava as shown below.

Based on the properties shown below, which material is most suitable for making a lava suit?

MaterialFlexibleStrongWaterproofHeat Conductivity
(1)P✓✓good
(2)Q✓✓✓poor
(3)R✓poor
(4)S✓✓good

Answer

Option 2. A lava suit must be flexible (so the scientist can move), strong (to withstand rough conditions), waterproof (to protect against splashes and steam), and have poor heat conductivity (to prevent heat from molten lava reaching the scientist). Material Q meets all four requirements: it is flexible, strong, waterproof, and a poor conductor of heat.

Question 14

2 marks

Ali received three parcels, D, H and L, that contained styrofoam balls, dish sponges and feathers respectively. He was also given a lever balance to compare the mass of parcels.

ParcelDHL
Parcel ContentsStyrofoam ballsDish spongesFeathers
Total Parcel Mass150 g300 g150 g

Which of the following diagrams best shows how the lever balance would look like?

(1)

(2)

(3)

(4)

Answer

Option 3. Parcel H (300 g) is heavier than both D and L (150 g each). D and L have the same mass. On a lever balance, H should be lower than D and L. D and L should balance each other. Option 3 correctly shows H on one side lower than D (or L) on the other, with D and L balanced when compared to each other.

Question 15

2 marks

Roy prepared four set-ups, A, B, C and D, using identical measuring cylinders each containing 20 cm³ of water. He placed identical corks and metal cubes in the four set-ups as shown below.

Based on the observation above, what is the volume of the cork and the metal cube respectively?

Volume of cork (cm³)Volume of metal cube (cm³)
(1)515
(2)535
(3)725
(4)1235

Answer

Option 1. The cork floats, so it displaces only the water equal to its submerged portion. By comparing the water levels in the different set-ups: the metal cube sinks and displaces water equal to its full volume. Reading off the differences in water levels between the set-ups gives the volume of cork = 5 cm³ and the volume of the metal cube = 15 cm³.

Question 16

2 marks

A beaker of boiling substance X was taken off the heat source at K and left to cool in the freezer. The changes in temperature of the substance X over time were recorded and shown in the graph below.

Based on the graph, which of the statement(s) about substance X below is/are true?

  • A  gaining heat at JK
  • B  in liquid state at KL
  • C  gaining heat at LM
  • D  in solid state at MN
  1. B only
  2. A and C only
  3. B, C and D only
  4. A, B and D only

Answer

Option 4. At JK the substance was boiling and being removed from heat — it was still losing heat (A says gaining at JK is wrong, but let us re-read: JK is before K where it was still on heat source — actually at JK it gains heat as it boils). At KL the temperature is constant — this is the freezing/condensation plateau so B (liquid state at KL) is true. At LM temperature continues to drop — the substance is losing heat (C is false). At MN the temperature is below the freezing plateau and the substance is in the solid state (D is true). Therefore A, B and D are true.

Question 17

2 marks

Sue set up an electrical circuit to find out the electrical conductivity of materials Q, R and S. All the components used in the circuit shown below were in working condition.

Sue observed that when the switch was closed, only bulbs B2 and B4 lit up.

Which of the following materials is/are insulator(s) of electricity?

  1. R only
  2. P and R only
  3. Q and S only
  4. P, Q and S only

Answer

Option 1. Since B2 and B4 lit up but B1 and B3 did not, the branches containing B1 and B3 are broken circuits. The materials in those broken branches are insulators. Based on the circuit diagram, material R is in the branch that prevents B1 and B3 from lighting. Materials Q and S allow current to flow (B2 and B4 light up). Therefore R is the only insulator.

Question 18

2 marks

Siti conducted an experiment with one of the four circuits, prepared using identical components, as shown below. She then recorded her results in a table as shown below.

Switch YSwitch ZNumber of bulbs lit
openclose2
closeopen1
closeclose2

Which of the following circuits did Siti use?

(1)

(2)

(3)

(4)

Answer

Option 1. When Switch Y is open and Z is closed: 2 bulbs light up. When Y is closed and Z is open: 1 bulb lights up. When both are closed: 2 bulbs light up. This pattern means Switch Z controls a parallel branch with 2 bulbs, and Switch Y controls one bulb that is bypassed when Z is closed. Circuit 1 matches this behaviour.

Question 19

2 marks

The diagrams, A and B, below show how a high speed train can move forward faster than a normal train. Diagram A shows the electromagnet under the train and on the train track. Diagram B shows the rows of magnets lined along the side of the train and inner side of the beam. The arrows show the directions of interactive magnetic force amongst the magnets as the train travels.

Which of the following explanations about the interaction amongst the magnets shown in diagrams A or B is correct?

DiagramPoles of electromagnet facing each otherResult of interaction between electromagnets
(1)ALike polesThey attracted to pull the train forward faster.
(2)ALike polesThey repelled to push the train off the ground to increase friction as the train moved forward faster.
(3)BLike poles / Unlike polesLike poles attracted to pull forward; Unlike poles repelled to push forward.
(4)BLike poles / Unlike polesLike poles repelled to push train forward; Unlike poles attracted to pull train forward.

Answer

Option 4. In Diagram B, the magnets are arranged along the side of the train. Like poles facing each other repel — this pushes the train forward. Unlike poles facing each other attract — this pulls the train forward. Both interactions work together to propel the train at high speed. Option 4 correctly describes this dual mechanism.

Question 20

2 marks

Raj dropped two identical pieces of paper, A and B, from the same height. He crushed paper A before he dropped them.

Paper A (crushed)

Paper B (flat)

Which of the following is likely to be the correct observation and the corresponding reason?

ObservationReason
(1)Paper A landed on the ground faster.It had more frictional force acting on it.
(2)Paper A landed on the ground slower.It had less exposed surface area.
(3)Paper B landed on the ground faster.It had greater exposed surface area.
(4)Paper B landed on the ground slower.It had more frictional force acting on it.

Answer

Option 4. Flat paper B has a greater exposed surface area, which means more air resistance (friction from air) acts on it as it falls. This greater air resistance slows down paper B, so it lands more slowly than the crushed paper A. Paper A (crushed) has a smaller surface area and less air resistance, so it falls faster. Option 4 correctly states that paper B lands slower because it has more frictional force (air resistance) acting on it.

Question 21

2 marks

The diagram below shows a hammock holding a baby that moves up and down from position A to B, and then from B to A repeatedly.

Which of the following statement(s) about the above hammock is/are true?

  • A  More gravitational force is acting on the baby at B than A.
  • B  Frictional force is acting on the baby as she moves from A to B.
  • C  Elastic spring force is pulling the baby in the opposite direction of her weight.
  • D  Elastic spring force acting on the hammock is increasing as it moves from A to B.
  1. B only
  2. A and D only
  3. B and C only
  4. A, C and D only

Answer

Option 3. B is true: frictional force (air resistance) acts on the baby as she moves from A to B. C is true: the elastic spring force pulls the hammock (and baby) upward, which is opposite to the direction of gravity (weight). A is false: gravitational force depends on mass, which does not change between A and B. D is false: as the hammock moves from A (bottom) to B (top), the spring is less stretched so elastic spring force decreases, not increases.

Question 22

2 marks

Anne made a paper butterfly. She attached the paper butterfly and an inflated balloon on the top and bottom of a straw respectively, as shown below. She then put a string through the straw before attaching it onto two poles.

Which one of the following explains how the paper butterfly moved when the air was released from the untied inflated balloon?

The force exerted by the moving air was ____________________.

  1. greater than the weight of the string
  2. less than the weight of the straw
  3. greater than the friction between the string and straw
  4. less than the friction between the balloon and string

Answer

Option 3. For the paper butterfly and straw to move along the string when air is released from the balloon, the thrust force produced by the escaping air must be sufficient to overcome the friction between the string and the straw. Therefore, the force exerted by the moving air must be greater than the friction between the string and the straw (Option 3).

Question 23

2 marks

An experiment was set up to find out the degree of transparency of four sheets of material A, B, C and D. Sheets A and B had cut-outs of different shapes, as shown below.

Which one of the following correctly describes the properties of sheets A, B, C and D?

Allows light to pass throughDoes not allow light to pass throughNot possible to tell
(1)DAB and C
(2)BA and CD
(3)BCA and D
(4)C and DBA

Answer

Option 2. Sheet B has a cut-out and the shadow on the screen shows the shape of the cut-out, meaning light passes through B. Sheet A has a cut-out but no shape appears in the shadow, meaning A blocks light. Sheet C also blocks light (its shadow is fully dark). Sheet D's transparency cannot be determined from the experiment alone. Therefore B allows light, A and C block light, and D is not possible to tell.

Question 24

2 marks

Peter made an object by attaching a wooden disc on a frosted rectangular block. He wanted to find out how the different positions, A and B, of the torch would affect the shadow of the object cast on the screen. The experimental set-up is shown below.

Which of the following shows the changes in the shadow of the object cast on the screen?

Shadow formed when torch was at position AShadow formed when torch was at position B
(1)Shadow A option 1Shadow B option 1
(2)Shadow A option 2Shadow B option 2
(3)Shadow A option 3Shadow B option 3
(4)Shadow A option 4Shadow B option 4

Answer

Option 3. The frosted block allows some light through (translucent), so it does not cast a completely dark shadow — its shadow appears lighter/grey. The wooden disc is opaque and casts a dark shadow of its circular shape. When the torch position changes from A to B, the position or size of the shadow shifts. Option 3 correctly shows a partial/grey shadow for the frosted block region and a solid dark circular shadow for the wooden disc in the correct positions for torch at A and B.

Question 25

2 marks

The diagram below shows an ice cube placed on a metal sheet.

After a while, Martha touched the ends of the metal sheet and her fingers felt cold.

Which one of the following is a possible reason for Martha's observation?

  1. Her fingers lost heat to the metal sheet.
  2. Her fingers gained heat from the ice cube.
  3. The ice cube lost heat to the metal sheet.
  4. The ice cube gained heat from the surrounding air.

Answer

Option 1. The metal sheet was cooled by the ice cube (the ice absorbed heat from the metal sheet via conduction). When Martha touched the cold metal sheet, heat flowed from her warmer fingers to the cooler metal sheet. Her fingers lost heat to the metal sheet, making them feel cold. This is why Option 1 is correct.

Question 26

2 marks

An experiment was conducted by placing two identical beakers each containing 100 ml of the same liquid in a room with a constant temperature of 25°C. The temperature of the liquid in each beaker was different.

After four hours, the temperature of the liquids in both beakers became the same and the volume of liquid left in each beaker was recorded in the table below.

BeakerInitial temperature (°C)Initial volume of liquid (ml)Volume of remaining liquid (ml)
AT10085
B7010070

Then a fan was placed above one of the beakers for six hours. The volume of liquid left in each beaker was recorded in the table below.

BeakerVolume of remaining liquid (ml)
A65
B65

Which of the following correctly identifies the beaker exposed to a fan and the initial temperature, T, of the liquid in beaker A?

BeakerT (°C)
(1)A40
(2)B40
(3)A80
(4)B80

Answer

Option 1. Beaker B started at 70°C (higher temperature) and lost 30 ml through evaporation without the fan. Beaker A lost only 15 ml without the fan, meaning A had a lower initial temperature T. After the fan was placed, both lost an additional 20 ml — but wait, Beaker A lost 20 ml (85→65) and Beaker B also lost 5 ml (70→65) in the fan phase. The fan must be over Beaker A (more evaporation). Since A evaporated less initially (15 ml vs 30 ml for B), T must be lower than 70°C, so T = 40°C. Therefore Beaker A had the fan and T = 40°C (Option 1).

Question 27

2 marks

The diagram below shows two toys, C and D, which are used to shoot a ping pong ball.

The ping pong ball in Toy C will shoot forward when the elastic band is released. The ping pong ball in Toy D will shoot forward when the handle is released.

Based on the information provided, which of the following graphs shows the relationship between the length of the pull and the elastic potential energy in both toys?

Toy CToy D
(1)Linear increaseLinear increase
(2)Linear increaseConstant (no change)
(3)Curved increase (non-linear)Linear increase
(4)Linear increaseCurved increase (non-linear)

Answer

Option 1. For both Toy C (elastic band) and Toy D (spring), as the length of pull increases, the elastic potential energy stored increases proportionally (linear relationship). Both an elastic band and a spring store more elastic potential energy the more they are stretched or compressed. The relationship for both is a straight line through the origin (linear increase).

Question 28

2 marks

The diagram below shows a man walking on a pavement lined with special tiles. During the day, when a person steps on the tiles, electricity is generated. The battery stores energy which is used to light up the street lamps at night.

Based on the information above, which of the following statement(s) is/are correct?

  • A  The amount of electricity stored in the battery increases, as the number of people walking on the pavement increases.
  • B  The amount of electrical energy generated increases, as the number of people walking on the pavement increases.
  • C  As the number of people walking on the pavement increases, the amount of chemical potential energy in the battery decreases.
  1. B only
  2. C only
  3. A and B only
  4. A, B, and C

Answer

Option 1. B is correct: more people stepping on the tiles means more kinetic energy converted to electrical energy, so more electrical energy is generated. A is incorrect: more people walking generates more electricity, but the battery stores the electricity — if electricity is being used to light lamps, the battery may not necessarily increase. C is incorrect: chemical potential energy in the battery increases (charges up) as more electricity is stored during the day. Only B is definitely correct.

Booklet B — Open-ended Questions (43 marks)

For questions 29 to 41, write your answers clearly in the spaces provided.

Question 29

3 marks

Sandra carried out an experiment by placing identical bread slices, A, B and C, into each sealed airtight bags before exposing each of them to different conditions as shown below.

Bread sliceNumber of drops of water addedLocation
A15freezer
B2enclosed cupboard
C15window sill
(a)

Which bread slices should Sandra use to find out if warmth is required for the growth of bread moulds? [1]

She observed the surface area of the bread slices covered with bread moulds over three days and recorded the results in the graph below.

(b)

Which of the lines, 1, 2 or 3, correctly represents the results observed on bread slice A? Give a reason for your answer. [2]

Answer

(a)

Sandra should use bread slices A and C. Both have the same amount of water (15 drops), so the variable being tested is warmth: slice A is in the freezer (cold) and slice C is on the window sill (warm). All other variables (water, type of bread) are kept the same.

(b)

Line 3. Bread slice A is in the freezer where the temperature is very low. Moulds need warmth to grow. Since the freezer is too cold, no mould will grow on bread slice A. Line 3 shows zero growth (flat at 0) throughout the three days, which correctly represents bread slice A.

Question 30

4 marks

Organisms X are commonly found on the flowers of plant R. The table shows some characteristics of plant R and organism X.

Plant ROrganism X
  • flowers contain either female or male parts
  • flowers give out a sweet-smelling scent
  • produces edible and fleshy fruits with many tiny seeds
  • has a hairy body
  • feeds on nectar
(a)

Describe how the organisms X can help to pollinate the plant R. [2]

(b)

Based on the information above, suggest a dispersal method for the fruit of plant R. Explain how the seeds of its fruit are being dispersed. [2]

Answer

(a)

Organism X (e.g. a bee) is attracted to the sweet-smelling scent of plant R's flowers and visits them to feed on nectar. As X moves around the flower, pollen grains from the anther (of a male flower) stick onto its hairy body. When X visits a female flower, the pollen grains are transferred from its body to the stigma of the female flower, thereby pollinating plant R.

(b)

Dispersal method: by animals. The fruit of plant R is edible and fleshy, so animals eat the fruit. The tiny seeds inside are indigestible and pass through the animal's digestive system unharmed. The seeds are then passed out in the animal's droppings, often far away from the parent plant, thus dispersing the seeds.

Question 31

3 marks

The diagram below shows two human systems namely system Z and the circulatory system. The arrows show the direction of movement of two gases entering and leaving the human systems.

(a)

Identify gas Q and system Z. [1]

(i) gas Q: ____________________

(ii) system Z: ____________________

(b)

Which part of the plant performs similar function as system Z? Explain your answer. [1]

(c)

Why is gas R important to plants? [1]

Answer

(a)(i)

Gas Q: Oxygen — oxygen is taken in by the circulatory system from the lungs (system Z) and transported around the body.

(ii) System Z: Respiratory system — system Z is the respiratory system, which is responsible for the exchange of gases (taking in oxygen and releasing carbon dioxide).

(b)

The stomata (in the leaves) of a plant perform a similar function to system Z (the respiratory system). The stomata allow gases to enter and leave the plant — taking in carbon dioxide and releasing oxygen during photosynthesis, and taking in oxygen and releasing carbon dioxide during respiration — just as the respiratory system exchanges gases in the human body.

(c)

Gas R is carbon dioxide. Carbon dioxide is important to plants because plants use it during photosynthesis to make food (glucose/sugar). Without carbon dioxide, plants cannot photosynthesise and will not be able to produce the food they need to survive and grow.

Question 32

3 marks

Siva connected a syringe to a container with a capacity of 300 cm³ as shown below.

(a)

After he had used two identical syringes and pumped 50 cm³ of air into the container one at a time, what would be the volume of air in the container? [1]

(b)

Would the mass of air in the container increase, decrease or remain the same after two pumps of air had entered the container? Give a reason for your answer. [1]

Siva inflated his boat and went for a full-day paddle on a hot day as shown below.

He was advised not to pump too much air while inflating the boat so that it would feel firm and not hard. It was to prevent the boat from tearing while he was paddling under the Sun.

(c)

Explain why there could be a possible tear in the over-inflated boat while he was paddling under the Sun. [1]

Answer

(a)

300 cm³. The container already has a capacity of 300 cm³ (full of air at atmospheric pressure). When you pump in more air, the air is compressed — the volume of air in the container remains 300 cm³ (the volume of the container does not change) but the air is now compressed. Note: if the question treats it as a rigid container being filled, the volume of the container stays at 300 cm³.

(b)

Increase. Air has mass. When more air is pumped into the container, more air particles are added to the same volume, so the total mass of air inside the container increases.

(c)

Under the hot Sun, the air inside the over-inflated boat gains heat from the Sun and expands. Since the boat is already over-inflated (too much air inside), the expanding air exerts even greater pressure on the walls of the boat. The walls of the boat cannot withstand the increased pressure, causing the boat to tear.

Question 33

3 marks

Doctors Peter and John, wearing spectacles made of identical materials, had a discussion in an air-conditioned room. It was observed that the inner surfaces of Doctor Peter's spectacle lenses fogged up each time he spoke but Doctor John's spectacle lenses remained clear throughout the discussion.

(a)

Explain why Doctor John's spectacle lenses did not fog up. [2]

When they moved to the outdoor garden to have their discussion, Doctor Peter observed that there was less fogging on the inner surfaces of his spectacle lenses compared to when he was in the air-conditioned room.

(b)

Explain his observation. [1]

Answer

(a)

Doctor John was wearing a mask that was taped securely across his nose bridge and cheeks. When he spoke, the warm, moist water vapour from his breath could not escape through the gaps around the nose bridge or cheeks and reach the cooler inner surface of his spectacle lenses. Since no warm water vapour came into contact with the cooler lens surface, there was no condensation, and his lenses did not fog up.

(b)

In the outdoor garden, the surrounding air temperature was higher (warmer) than inside the air-conditioned room. The spectacle lenses were at a higher temperature (closer to the temperature of the water vapour from his breath). The difference in temperature between the warm moist breath and the lens surface was smaller, so the water vapour lost less heat when it contacted the lenses and condensed less. This resulted in less fogging on Doctor Peter's spectacle lenses outdoors.

Question 34

3 marks

Siti designed a Ball Toss Game for her school carnival as shown below.

Ball Toss Game

She wanted to set up an electrical circuit in the Ball Toss Game using the following working components: 3 bulbs, 3 switches, 3 batteries, wires, and a music box.

Each bulb, X, Y and Z, had to be controlled by a separate switch found under each target. If either target B or C was hit, the switch would close and only the bulb next to it would light up.

If target A was hit, the switch would close and only bulb X would light up and the music box would play a victory tune at the same time.

Based on the information given, complete the circuit diagram below. [3]

Answer

The completed circuit has three separate (parallel) branches, each with its own battery and switch:

  • Branch for Target A: Battery → Switch A → Bulb X AND Music Box (in series) → back to battery. When Switch A closes, both Bulb X and the music box are powered.
  • Branch for Target B: Battery → Switch B → Bulb Y → back to battery. When Switch B closes, only Bulb Y lights up.
  • Branch for Target C: Battery → Switch C → Bulb Z → back to battery. When Switch C closes, only Bulb Z lights up.

Each branch is independent (parallel) so hitting one target does not affect the others. The music box is in series with Bulb X in the Target A branch so both activate together.

Question 35

3 marks

Amy bought a facial mask set which consisted of a mask cream containing a metal-based substance which helped to cleanse her face. She used the handheld mask-removal tool with Plate X, held at a distance from her face to remove the mask as shown below.

(a)

Based on the information above, what could Plate X be? [1]

(b)

Amy's friend told her that the metal-based substance added to the mask cream could be made of silver. Do you agree with Amy's friend? Explain why. [1]

Amy then bought a different handheld mask removal tool and used the same mask cream of an identical amount to cleanse her face. She held the new handheld mask-removal tool at the same distance away from her face. She observed that it removed less mask from her face within the same period of time.

(c)

Give a reason for her observation made when the new handheld mask removal tool was used. [1]

Answer

(a)

Plate X is a magnet. It can attract the metal-based substance in the mask cream from a distance, allowing the mask to be removed without direct contact with the face.

(b)

No, I do not agree. Silver is not a magnetic material (it is not attracted to magnets). If the metal-based substance were silver, Plate X (a magnet) would not be able to attract it and remove the mask from Amy's face. Since the mask-removal tool works by magnetic attraction, the metal-based substance must be a magnetic material such as iron.

(c)

The magnet in the new handheld mask-removal tool was weaker (had a weaker magnetic force) than the original tool. With a weaker magnetic force, it could attract less of the metal-based substance in the mask cream over the same period of time, resulting in less mask being removed.

Question 36

3 marks

Joe conducted an experiment using the set-up as shown below.

From the same starting point on the ramp, he released boxes A, B and C, made of identical materials, one at a time. Then he measured the time taken for each box to reach the ground.

BoxMass (g)Area of contact with the ramp (cm²)Time taken for box to reach the ground (s)
A8503
B41506
C81003
(a)

Based on the results above, did the area of contact with the ramp affect the amount of friction acting on the box? Explain your answer. [1]

Snow chains are fitted to the tyres of vehicles during winter as a safety measure while driving on icy road surfaces as shown below.

(b)

Explain how the snow-chained tyres act as a safety measure while the vehicles are driven on icy road surfaces. [2]

Answer

(a)

No, the area of contact did not affect the amount of friction. Boxes A and C have the same mass (8 g) but different areas of contact (50 cm² vs 100 cm²), yet they both took the same time (3 s) to reach the ground. This shows that changing the area of contact did not change the frictional force acting on the boxes.

(b)

Icy roads are very smooth and slippery, resulting in very little friction between the tyres and the road. Snow chains fitted to tyres increase the roughness of the contact between the tyres and the icy road surface. This increases the frictional force between the tyres and the road, preventing the vehicle's wheels from skidding or sliding. The greater friction allows the driver to maintain better control of the vehicle, making it safer to drive on icy roads.

Question 37

3 marks

Tony prepared a ball of dough to make a pizza. He took a rolling pin to flatten the ball of dough as shown in the diagram below.

(a)

State the effect of the force exerted by the rolling pin on the ball of dough. [1]

Then Tony tossed the flattened dough into the air to stretch it further to increase its size as shown in the diagram below.

(b)

Draw a line graph in the space below to show the amount of gravitational force acting on the flattened dough as it was being tossed into the air. [1]

(c)

Explain, in terms of forces, how the flattened dough was able to move up into the air when Tony tossed it. [1]

Answer

(a)

The force exerted by the rolling pin changed the shape of the ball of dough by flattening it (from a ball shape into a flat, disc-like shape).

(b)

The graph should show a horizontal straight line (constant value) for gravitational force against height as the dough is tossed into the air. This is because the gravitational force depends only on the mass of the dough, which does not change as the dough moves up or down. Therefore, gravitational force remains constant at all heights.

(c)

When Tony tossed the dough upward, he exerted a pushing force (upward) on the dough. This upward pushing force was greater than the gravitational force (weight) pulling the dough downward. Since the upward force exceeded the downward gravitational force, the net force was upward, causing the dough to move up into the air.

Question 38

3 marks

Mary wanted to find out if the presence of substance A will affect the degree of transparency of a liquid. She prepared the set-up using 400 ml of liquid X and conducted the experiment in a dark room as shown below.

(a)

Describe how Mary should prepare a control set-up for the experiment. [1]

(b)

Explain why Mary conducted the experiment in a dark room. [1]

(c)

Mary wanted to compare how the presence of different substances would affect the degree of transparency of a liquid. She repeated the experiment by replacing substance A with B using 400 ml of liquid Y instead. Her classmate commented that it was not a fair test. Do you agree? Explain your answer. [1]

Answer

(a)

The control set-up should be identical to the experimental set-up, but without substance A. Mary should use 400 ml of the same liquid X in the same container, with the torch at the same position and the light sensor at the same position, but with no substance added to the liquid.

(b)

Mary conducted the experiment in a dark room to ensure that all other sources of light (besides the torch) were eliminated. This way, the only light reaching the light sensor came from the torch through the liquid, making the readings accurate. Other sources of light could affect the amount of light detected by the light sensor and give inaccurate results.

(c)

Yes, I agree that it was not a fair test. Mary changed two variables at once: she replaced substance A with substance B, AND she changed the liquid from liquid X to liquid Y. To make it a fair test, only one variable should be changed at a time. The type of liquid should have been kept the same (liquid X) while only the substance was changed from A to B.

Question 39

4 marks

Ben could not open the glass jar with a tight lid hence he placed the jar into a tub of hot water as shown below.

(a)

After five minutes, Ben still could not open the glass jar.

(i)

Without using different apparatus or additional materials, suggest what Ben could do in order to open the glass jar. [1]

(ii)

Explain your answer in (a)(i). [2]

(b)

Two identical metal cups were stuck together and needed to be separated. Put a tick (✓) in the correct box(es) to indicate which of the following is/are needed to separate the cups most quickly. [1]

Items neededTick (✓)
Ice cubes
Hot water
Tap water

Answer

(a)(i)

Ben should place the metal lid (only) into the hot water instead of the whole jar. He could turn the jar upside down so that the metal lid is submerged in the hot water while the glass jar body is held above the water.

(a)(ii)

When the metal lid is placed in the hot water, it gains heat from the hot water and its temperature rises. Metal expands more than glass when heated. The metal lid will therefore expand more than the glass jar neck, causing the lid to become slightly looser. This loosens the tight lid and makes it easier for Ben to open the glass jar.

(b)

The items needed are: Ice cubes ✓ and Hot water ✓

To separate the stuck metal cups most quickly: pour hot water on the outer cup (so it expands and becomes larger) and apply ice cubes to the inner cup (so it contracts and becomes smaller). This combination of expansion and contraction creates the greatest size difference between the two cups, separating them most quickly.

Question 40

4 marks

Tom and Judy prepared identical hot moist towels, A and B, respectively to welcome the hotel guests. Tom rolled up towels A while Judy laid towels B flat as shown in the diagram below.

(a)

Five minutes later, Tom observed that the towel from the top of the stack of towels B was much cooler than one of the rolled-up towels on the top stack of towels A. Explain the observation. [1]

To keep the hot moist towels A hot, Judy suggested storing them in a covered box before distributing to the guests. They put towels A in two different boxes made of metal and wood respectively as shown below. They measured the temperature of towels A in both boxes after fifteen minutes.

They recorded the results in the table below.

Time (min)(b)(i) _____ box(b)(ii) _____ box
05555
154048
(b)

Based on the information, fill in the words 'metal' and 'wooden' in the correct blanks in the result table above. [1]

(c)

Explain your answer in (b)(ii). [2]

Answer

(a)

The flat towels B have a greater exposed surface area in contact with the cooler surrounding air compared to the rolled-up towels A. A greater exposed surface area means more heat is lost to the surrounding cooler air per unit time. Therefore, the flat towels B lose heat faster and become cooler than the rolled-up towels A.

(b)

(b)(i): Metal box (temperature dropped from 55°C to 40°C — greater heat loss)
(b)(ii): Wooden box (temperature dropped from 55°C to 48°C — less heat loss)

(c)

The wooden box is a poor conductor of heat (wood has poor heat conductivity). Heat from towel A is conducted through the wooden walls of the box slowly to the surrounding cooler environment. This means less heat escapes from the wooden box, so the towels inside the wooden box retained more heat and remained at a higher temperature (48°C) after 15 minutes compared to the metal box (40°C).

Question 41

4 marks

Gabby prepared a set-up as shown below.

(a)

State the main energy conversion as the water falls from the container A. [2]

(b)

Water was splashing off the water wheel and making a mess, so Gabby adjusted the retort stand to lower the spout of Container A closer to the water wheel. She noticed that the water wheel was still spinning, but the light bulb became dimmer.

Explain why the bulb became dimmer. [2]

Answer

(a)

As the water falls from Container A, the following energy conversions take place:

Gravitational potential energy (of water in Container A) → Kinetic energy (of falling water) → Kinetic energy (of spinning water wheel) → Electrical energy (in the generator) → Light energy (in the bulb)

(b)

When Gabby lowered the spout of Container A closer to the water wheel, the water fell from a lower height. The water in Container A had less gravitational potential energy (because it was at a lower position/height). This meant less gravitational potential energy was converted to kinetic energy of the falling water, so the water hit the water wheel with less kinetic energy. The water wheel spun with less kinetic energy, generating less electrical energy in the generator. Less electrical energy resulted in less light energy in the bulb, causing the bulb to become dimmer.
